text: There is a troubling asymmetry at the heart of inattentional blindness: we do not know what we are missing. The gorilla-blind observer does not experience a gap or a flicker of uncertainty — they watch the same video as the gorilla-aware observer and feel equally confident about what they saw. Daniel Kahneman calls this 'what you see is all there is': the mind constructs a coherent story from available inputs and presents it as a complete picture. The invisible gorilla is not absent from the observer's memory; it was never a candidate for memory in the first place. This is why self-assessment of attentional lapses is so unreliable: the very mechanism that produces the lapse also prevents its detection.
question: According to the passage, why is self-assessment of attentional lapses unreliable?

options.text: People are embarrassed to admit missing important stimuli and underreport lapses
Attention lapses occur too briefly for any subsequent reflection to recover them
The mechanism that produces the lapse also prevents its detection, leaving no felt gap
Working memory erases traces of unattended stimuli within seconds of the lapse
